title: Local Experiences, Search, and Spillovers in the Housing Market
abstract: Recent local price growth explains differences in search behavior across prospective homebuyers. Those experiencing higher growth in their postcode of residence search more broadly across locations and house characteristics, without changing attention devoted to individual sales listings, and have shorter search duration. Effects are stronger for homeowners, in particular those living in less wealthy areas and looking for a new primary residence. We use reduced-form analysis and a quantitative equilibrium model to show that the expansion of search breadth translates into widespread spillovers onto house sales prices and inventories of listings across postcodes within a metropolitan area.

article.introduction:
Personal experiences of returns and price growth shapehouseholds' trading behavior and explain differences in portfolio decisions (Malmendier and Nagel (2011,2016), Giglio et al. (2020), and D'Acunto et al. (2021)). In the housing market, local price fluctuations impact homebuyers by influencing views on future aggregate and local price growth (see Armona, Fuster, and Zafar (2019) and Kuchler and Zafar (2019)) and by impacting local homeowners' home equity, which, when owners face collateral and liquidity constraints, affects their ability and willingness to finance the purchase of a new house (Stein (1995) and Fuster and Zafar (2016)).
  To study the real effects of realized price growth on housing markets, it is crucial to understand its impact on buyers' search behavior. Housing is a search market that is highly segmented and illiquid. Since search effort and behavior determine the speed and quality of matches between buyers and house listings, changes in homebuyers' beliefs and wealth are transmitted to sales prices and transaction volume through their effect on search. However, the different aspects of buyers' search—the choice of browsed listings, the effort allocated to inspecting individual listings, and the length of the search process—are rarely observed.1
  In this paper, we exploit a new data set that tracks online house search activity in Australia to study how the search behavior of prospective homebuyers responds to local (postcode-level) realized price growth, and through what channels experiences influence behavior. We then explore the real effects on sales prices and on the inventory of houses for sale. We find that buyers experiencing higher price growth search more broadly across locations and house characteristics and have shorter search durations, resulting in faster matches with listings. The increase in search breadth and the decrease in search duration generate spillover effects leading to higher prices and lower inventories in other neighborhoods of the same metropolitan area. We study these spillovers using both reduced-form analysis and the segmented search model of Piazzesi, Schneider, and Stroebel (2020), calibrated to our data. The two approaches produce results of the same sign and magnitude.
  Our data set contains information on the interactions between individual users, who self-identify as actively looking for a new house, and online listings from the largest Australian property website. Crucially, we observe users' current postcode of residence, their homeownership status, and whether they are looking for a new primary residence or an investment property, as well as detailed information on listings of properties for sale, including locations, dwelling characteristics, and listing and sales prices.
  We begin our analysis by exploring how the search behavior of prospective homebuyers responds to price growth in their postcode of residence. Theoretical work on search over discrete sets (see Caplin (2016) and Caplin, Dean, and Leahy (2019)) shows that agents optimally trade off the costs and benefits of search across two dimensions: the effort allocated to collect information on individual items (in the housing market, individual listings) and the breadth of alternatives considered (“the consideration set”).2It is not clear ex ante which dimension should be more affected by an increase in search effort, or how an increase in search effort along either dimension will impact search duration or the rate at which buyers match with listings.
  Figure1visually summarizes the relation between recent (over the past two years) postcode-level price growth and different dimensions of search behavior of prospective homebuyers. Local price growth is positively correlated with the number of listings (top left panel), postcodes (top right panel), and market segments3(bottom left panel) considered by prospective buyers. In contrast, there is only a weak (andnegative) correlation with the amount of time allocated to browsing each listing (bottom right panel). These patterns suggest that positive price growth in the postcode of residence increases the perceived benefits of search effort, and that home buyers believe that expanding the consideration set is more valuable than increasing the amount of attention allocated to individual listings. Panel regressions estimated at the user level, which include time × area fixed effects and postcode or even individual user fixed effects, provide results consistent with Figure1. A one-standard-deviation (15%) increase in experienced price growth over the previous two years leads to a 6% increase in the number of listings visited over the month. The effect is of similar magnitude when the dependent variable is the number of postcodes or market segments explored. There is no significant effect, however, on the amount of time spent browsing each individual listing.4
  Broader searches increase the number of explored properties over time, and thus may increase the speed at which buyers find suitable matches. Within traditional theoretical frameworks of housing search, this can be interpreted as akin to an increase in search intensity, which would lead to faster matches between prospective buyers and listings and in turn to shorter search durations. However, broader searches may also potentially lead to longer search spells and lower matching frequency. By increasing the consideration set, buyers might allocate excessive time and attention to less relevant properties and might fail to narrow their focus onto properties that present better matches. To disentangle the two alternative mechanisms, we test the effects of higher realized price growth on search duration and find that a one-standard-deviation increase in realized local price growth leads to a 15% reduction in search duration,5which is consistent with the view that broader searches are associated with faster matches between buyers and listings.
  We also explore the effects of realized price growth on the likelihood of households entering active house search by studying the relationship between the timing of searchers' entry into our sample and local historical price growth. Unlike the behavior of active searchers, the number of users starting their search is sensitive to aggregate (metropolitan area level or rural area level) realized price growth but not to realized price growth in their postcode of residence. Area-level price fluctuations may have a stronger association with the decision to start a search due to their relationship with underlying economic fundamentals (see, e.g., Head, Huw, and Sun (2014)) or due to their effects on beliefs, particularly for current renters, as we discuss below (Agarwal, Hu, and Huang (2016)).6
  In the second part of the paper, we exploit heterogeneity in users' characteristics to test the predictions of two key channels—extrapolative expectations and collateral constraints, or home equity—that can explain the relationship between experienced price growth and behavior.
  We begin by comparing the behavior of homeowners and renters. If the extrapolative expectations channel is the only mechanism explaining users' behavior, we would expect a negative or muted response of homeowners' searches to higher local price growth. In fact, if local (postcode-specific) price growth is extrapolated to predict higher local growth, beyond that of the rest of the surrounding area, homeowners will have an incentive to slow down or delay their house search until the higher appreciation of their house has been realized (see the evidence from the field experiments developed by Bottan and Perez-Truglia (2020)). If postcode-specific growth is used to extrapolate future aggregate or metropolitan (or rural) area-level growth (Kuchler and Zafar (2019)), homeowners have no clear motive to search either more or less intensively, since their house already offers a hedge against aggregate price fluctuations (Sinai and Souleles (2005)). For renters, the extrapolative expectations channel would instead predict a positive response. When expecting high price growth, renters will feel pressure to find a matching house quickly, before ownership becomes less affordable (the so-called “fear of missing out”). Renters will respond more strongly if they interpret local price growth as a signal of aggregate price growth, since they will then fear being priced out of the entire market, and less strongly if they instead interpret higher postcode-specific price growth as simply a signal of higher future postcode growth.
  We find that price growth specific to a certain postcode has a statistically significant and positive effect on the breadth of searches (consideration sets) for homeowners in the postcode. This result is at odds with the extrapolation channel being the only driver of search behavior. For renters, we find point estimates that are positive but 50% smaller than for homeowners, and not statistically significant. However, these magnitudes are still economically significant and the sign is consistent with renters extrapolating future metro area price growth from historical local price growth. When looking at the likelihood of starting a house search, we find that the number of renters joining our data set is also positively associated with historical price growth in the metropolitan (or rural) area in which they live. This result is consistent with renters extrapolating higher aggregate (or area-level) price growth from recent area-level price growth.
  For homeowners, the positive response to experienced price growth seems to suggest a role for the collateral constraints, or home equity, channel. Since homeowners may use equity in their current house to finance their future down payment, postcode-level growth increases their ability to finance a new purchase and thus potentially their willingness and urgency to find a new house (Fuster and Zafar (2016,2019)). To pin down the role of collateral constraints, we focus on homeowners who are more likely to be financially constrained, and thus more likely to need equity in their current house to finance a new purchase. We first show that the effects of local price growth are driven by homeowners living in “lower wealth” postcodes (i.e., with prices below the median of the metropolitan area). We next show that the effects are highly significant when focusing on homeowners who are looking for a new house to occupy (and thus who are likely to be selling their primary residence at the same time), but insignificant for homeowners who are looking for an investment house or a new secondary residence. Finally, owners older than 65 are less responsive than younger owners. Expected retirement age is approximately 65 years old in Australia, and retirees are unlikely to be moving up the housing ladder. Moreover, they usually have low leverage and high equity in their current home, which makes them less sensitive to recent price appreciation.
  Even if the results above suggest an important role for the collateral constraints channel, we still cannot completely rule out that the expectations channel may also partially affect the behavior of homeowners. Owners who are more prone to extrapolation might also be more likely to be constrained through high-loan-to-value mortgages (Bailey et al. (2019)), and low-income and low-wealth households, who are more likely to be constrained, might have systematically different beliefs than middle class or wealthy households (Das, Kuhnen, and Nagel (2020)).7
  In the last part of the paper, we study whether the expansion of search breadth, driven by higher experienced price growth, generates spillover effects onto house sales in other parts of a metropolitan area. The housing literature typically studies spillovers induced by geographic proximity, for instance, between competing properties for sales that are located within a narrow distance radius.8We show that spillover effects operating through the behavior of local homebuyers are transmitted through the breadth of buyers' searches and can affect houses listed for sale across the entire metropolitan area. Our data set can identify these effects because we observe both the postcodes from which searches originate and the postcodes to which they are directed.
  We begin our analysis by constructing reduced-form tests that track spillover effects within metropolitan areas. However, the reduced-form tests need to overcome important identification challenges, particularly when evaluating spillover effects on prices. Users who have experienced higher price growth in their home postcode and who have larger search breadth may be systematically more likely to visit higher-quality properties. To address this issue, we construct realized price growth for the sample of users who areexpected$expected$to browse a listing based on its location.9
  We next use realized price growth for theexpected$expected$sample of visitors to predict the average search breadth of users visiting a listing and to estimate the effects on sales prices using two-stage least squares. We find that after controlling for postcode and metro area by year-month fixed effects, a 10% increase in search breadth induced by higher experienced price growth for theexpected$expected$sample of visitors coincides with a 2% to 2.5% higher sales price. For a house of average price in our sample, this amounts to roughly 15,000 to 18,750 Australian dollars, or approximately 12,000 to 15,000 U.S. dollars.10This result is robust to controlling for a wide range of listing characteristics (location, number of bedrooms, bathrooms, etc.), and remains significant after controlling for the last available asking price for the listing.11
  We construct an analogous specification for the change in inventory associated with broader search breadth. For this second set of tests, we construct a panel that tracks the inventory of properties for sale in each postcode and month in our data set. We find that a 10% increase in search breadth induced by higher realized price growth (for theexpected$expected$sample of visitors) translates into a roughly 6% drop in inventory.
  To establish whether the magnitude of the effects measured in reduced form is economically sensible, we turn to the quantitative equilibrium framework developed by Piazzesi, Schneider, and Stroebel (2020), which features segmented search and heterogeneous search breadth across homebuyers. We first calibrate the model to match buyers' search ranges, postcode-level market activity (e.g., turnover, supply of listings, and time on the market), and prices separately for Melbourne and Sydney, which are the largest housing markets in Australia. We then study the effects of an increase in search breadth on market outcomes in the cross section of postcodes. A 5% increase in search breadth across the entire metropolitan area, matched by a roughly 15% drop in search duration (changes that are roughly equal to the effects on search breadth and duration of a one-standard-deviation increase in realized price growth), leads to average increases in postcode-level prices of roughly 1.1% in Melbourne and 1.9% in Sydney and to average decreases in postcode-level inventories of listings for sale of 14.4% and 15.6%, respectively. Moreover, the model generates a positive (negative) relation between the increase in search breadth for the buyers browsing a specific postcode and changes in prices (inventory) in that specific postcode. Within Sydney, a postcode that experiences a 10% higher increase in the search breadth of its visitors has a 1.5% larger increase in prices and a 6% larger drop in inventory. Overall, the results from the model line up with the reduced-form estimates from the data, with remarkably close magnitudes.
  The rest of the paper is organized as follows. SectionIpresents our data set and the measures of search behavior that we use in our analysis. SectionIIstudies the response of buyers' search behavior to realized price growth in their postcode of residence. SectionIIIinvestigates the economic mechanism driving the response. SectionIVanalyzes the extent to which the response of search behavior to local price growth generates spillovers on prices and inventories in other parts of a metropolitan area. SectionVconcludes.
article.I. Online Real Estate Advertising Data S:
The proprietary data set used in this study is made available to us by realestate.com.au (REA), Australia's largest property website and apps suite.12Three key features of this data set make it uniquely suited to studying the effects of local realized price growth on homebuyers' search behavior.
  First, the data set contains detailed information on user activity over time and across listings, as well as detailed information on browsed listings. For a random sample of approximately 9,000 users, who self-identify as interested in purchasing a property,13we observe logins to the website, the listings they browse, how many times they visit each listing, and the amount of time spent browsing each listing on a daily basis. This data set covers the period from January 1, 2017 to April 30, 2019, for a total of approximately 3 million unique user × day × listing observations. In addition to information on demand-side behavior, the property website provides information on listings. For each listing, we observe the listing date, the type of listing, and information on the associated property characteristics: property type (whether house/townhouse, unit, land, or other), postcode, asking price, number of bedrooms, number of bathrooms, number of parking spots, and size. For listings associated with properties sold over the period spanned by our study, the data set provides the sale date and the sales price.
  Second, the data include information on user characteristics. We observe the postcode in which a user currently resides, whether she owns a property, her age, her gender, and whether she is searching for a property to occupy or for a second house or an investment property.
  Third, the data set covers all of Australia. Figure2displays using red dots the centroids of postcodes for which at least one user is included in our data. The majority of users are located in two coastal regions (the southeast and east, and the southwest) and in clusters corresponding to the eight state capital cities (Adelaide, Brisbane, Canberra, Darwin, Hobart, Melbourne, Perth, and Sydney). On average, over a period of one year, there are seven active users per postcode, and the number of active users over the year is greater than or equal to five in half of the postcodes in our data set. This broad coverage allows us to explore prospective homebuyers' behavior across a wide range of local markets, and for different values of recent price growth over time within the same local market. As displayed in Figure IA.3 of theInternet Appendix, Sydney, Melbourne, and Brisbane—the three most active cities—experienced high price growth over the four years prior to the start of our sample and peaked, respectively, around July 2017, December 2017, and April 2018.14Hobart and Adelaide had positive growth over the entire period covered by our sample, while Darwin and Perth—whose economies are tightly linked to the mining and commodities sectors—are at the opposite end of the spectrum in that they had falling prices since 2014.
  SectionIof theInternet Appendixcontains several tests showing that (i) our data set is representative of Australian homebuyers and of the total stock of listings, and (ii) many aspects of Australian housing markets are comparable to those of U.S. housing markets.
  Households searching for a new house need to choose how to allocate their effort across two main dimensions. First, they need to select the breadth of listings to explore, that is, their consideration set. Second, they need to allocate time and attention to each individual listing.
  We measure the breadth of users' consideration sets by tracking the number of unique listings, postcodes, and “segments” browsed in a month. Our definition of segments is based on postcode, property type (house or apartment unit), and number of bedrooms, reflecting the fact that a broader search may cover not only more locations, but also a wider set of characteristics. To construct segments, we split all listings into groups based on postcode and then divide the groups within each postcode into eight subcategories: one, two, three, and four or more bedrooms, separately for houses and apartment units. We measure the number of segments visited by useriin monthtusingNumSegi,t=∑τ∈tIi,ℓ∈(post,nbed,type),τ$\textit{NumSe}{g}_{i,t}={\sum}_{\tau \in t}{I}_{i,\ell \in (\textit{post},\textit{nbed},\textit{type}),\tau}$, whereIi,ℓ∈(.),τ${I}_{i,\ell \in (.),\tau}$is a dummy equal to one if useriin montht(in any day τ included in montht) visits at least one listing (ℓ) in a certain segment.
  To instead track the effort and attention allocated to individual listings, we calculate the number of minutes and the number of visits per listing in a month. Finally, to capture the extent to which attention is concentrated (or allocated uniformly) across listings browsed in a month, we calculate theHerfindahl indexof time spent per listing.
  TableIreports summary statistics. The table reports the mean, median, standard deviation and four percentiles (5th, 25th, 75th, and 95th) of the average of each measure across users. The average user browses 42 listings, nine postcodes, and 14 segments in a month. There is substantial heterogeneity across users, as shown by the high cross-sectional standard deviations. The 95thpercentile of users based on search activity browses on average 151 listings, 29 postcodes, and 47 segments per month. Turning to the measures capturing attention per listing, the average user visits the listings in her consideration set 2.40 times in a month and allocates about four minutes per listing, while the Herfindahl index indicates that the average user allocates her time quite evenly across listings.
  The last panel reports summary statistics for search duration, measured as the time elapsed between the first and last interactions with any listing in the data set for each user. While all of the users included in the data set are actively searching for a house, we cannot be sure whether users ending their search have matched with a property. However, for a limited subset of users, REA provides a flag identifying whether they are likely to have bought a house at the end of their search.15In the table, we report statistics of search duration for both the entire sample and the restricted sample of users explicitly identified as “likely buyers.” For the former, the mean search duration is nine months, while for the latter it is 10 months. Overall, the distribution of search durations is similar across the two groups.
article.II. Local Price Growth and Search Behavi:
In this section, we focus on the relationship between price growth in the postcode of residence and the search behavior of prospective homebuyers. Local price growth can affect housing demand through prospective homebuyers' beliefs and collateral constraints.16Armona, Fuster, and Zafar (2019) show that information on recent local (postcode-level) price changes affects views on future local price growth at both short and long horizons, while Kuchler and Zafar (2019) find that local price growth experienced by U.S. households affects their views on future price growth at the national level. Moreover, local price growth has a direct effect on local homeowners' wealth and their ability to lever up, by increasing the price of their current house and the value of their home equity (see Stein (1995) and Ortalo-Magne and Rady (2006)). Experimental evidence by Fuster and Zafar (2016) shows that households that observe positive wealth shocks are more willing to purchase a new house and make larger down payments.
  However, when considering the effects on search behavior, it is not clear which aspect of homebuyers' searches will be most sensitive to local price fluctuations. To the extent that realized price growth will lead to greater search effort, theoretical work on search over discrete sets (see Caplin (2016) and Caplin, Dean, and Leahy (2019)) suggests that searchers will optimally trade off the costs and benefits of attention across two dimensions: the effort allocated to collect information on individual properties, and the breadth of alternatives considered (i.e., the consideration set). An increase in search intensity across either margin may also affect search duration. In particular, if we interpret an increase in the number of properties explored (the search breadth, or consideration set) as an increase in search intensity, theoretical models of one- and two-sided search predict that higher search intensity will lead to shorter search durations and faster matches with listings (Morgan and Manning (1985) and Wheaton (1990)). On the other hand, both broader search ranges and greater attention per listing could potentially lead to longer search spells and a lower matching frequency because buyers might end up allocating excessive time and attention to less relevant properties, and hence may fail to focus their effort on properties that present valuable matches.
  While the effects on the behavior of active searchers are our main focus, and are discussed in SectionsII.AandII.C, higher realized price growth can also influence the decision to initiate the search process, for both current homeowners dissatisfied with their properties and renters transitioning into homeownership. We devote SectionII.Dto this last analysis.
  Figure1provides preliminary evidence on the relationship between postcode-level price growth (on thex-axis) and different aspects of search behavior (y-axis) for prospective homebuyers residing in the postcode.17Price growth is strongly correlated with the number of listings browsed in the month (top left panel), and even more strongly with the number of postcodes (top right panel) and the number of market segments considered (bottom left panel). In contrast, there is only a weak andnegativerelationship with the time allocated to browsing each individual listing.
  TableIIdisplays estimates of the effect of price growth (the coefficient β in equation (1)) on the breadth of consideration sets. Panel A (B) (C) reports estimates for the log of the number of listings (postcodes) (segments) browsed. The specifications in the first and second columns include postcode fixed effects, while those in the third and fourth columns include individual user fixed effects. The specifications in the first and third columns include year-month fixed effects, while the specifications in the second and fourth columns contain year-month × area fixed effects. Thus, the fourth column presents results from the tightest specification, which coincides with equation (1). Panel regression estimates confirm the evidence presented in Figure1and are remarkably stable across specifications. A one-standard-deviation increase in price growth in the postcode of residence (i.e., 15%) over the previous two years coincides with an approximate 6% increase in the number of listings browsed. We find similar results when the dependent variable is the number of postcodes or the number of segments covered by house searches over a one-month period.
  TableIIIhas the same layout as TableII, but focuses on the effects of realized price growth on attention per listing. Panel A (B) displays results for the log of the number of minutes (visits) per listing over the month. Point estimates of these effects are economically small and not statistically significant. Even though average attention per listing remains unchanged, prospective buyers may still be skewing attention allocation toward specific listings in response to realized price growth. To test this hypothesis, in Panel C the dependent variable is the Herfindahl index for the time allocated to individual listings over the month. Using this dependent variable, the effect of price growth is again statistically insignificant.
  The evidence on the increase in search breadth, together with the unchanged attention per listing, indicates that higher realized price growth leads to an overall increase in time allocated to house search. TableIA.IIin theInternet Appendixshows this directly. We use the regression specification in equation (1) to estimate the effects of realized price growth on time spent on the website, and on the total number of pages browsed on the websitepermonth$per\nobreakspace month$. For both variables, the effect is positive and highly statistically significant.
  We conduct several robustness tests to confirm that our findings are indeed driven by the response of local homebuyers to realized price growth in their postcode of residence, and not instead by alternative factors.
  First, the search effort of local residents may directly influence price growth in their postcode of residence, leading to reverse causality. We believe this is unlikely to be the case, since the median (average) fraction of listings browsed by prospective buyers in their postcode of residence is only 7% (16%). Nevertheless, to dispel this concern we develop an empirical strategy that uses local land supply elasticity as an IV.21We discuss the construction of the instrument and our IV results in SectionIIIof theInternet Appendix. Our instrumented estimates of the effects of realized price growth on search breadth are highly statistically significant and larger than the OLS estimates discussed in the previous section. Moreover, the instrumented estimates of the effects of price growth on attention per listing remain economically small and statistically insignificant.
  Second, another concern when interpreting the results in the previous section is that homebuyers who have observed higher price growth may be including in otherwise unchanged search ranges a few outlier houses from high-price locations, even though the likelihood they will buy these properties is quite low. If so, the expansion in search ranges should coincide with a sharp increase in the dispersion of explored prices, driven mainly by the right tail of the distribution. Because it is not clear whether such behavior should be interpreted as a genuine increase in search effort, we develop a test to rule it out as a driver of our results.22We first collect the median price (which we use as proxy for the overall price level) for each postcode explored by the users in each month. We then compute the standard deviation and the quantiles of prices across the postcodes explored by each user in each month. Finally, we estimate the same specification as in equation (1). We report the results in TableIA.III. Panel A shows that the relationship between the breadth (the standard deviation) of the explored price levels across postcodes and realized price growth in the postcode of residence is not statistically significant after including area × month fixed effects. In Panels B through E, we find no evidence of a stronger effect on the right tail of the range of explored prices. Rather, a one-standard-deviation increase in price growth uniformly shifts upward (by approximately 1.5% to 2%) the 10th, 25th, 75th, and 90thquantiles of the distribution. Thus, our results are not driven by the inclusion of a few outlier houses located in expensive areas. Moreover, the nearly uniform shift of the entire distribution of browsed prices suggests that users who have observed higher price growth, shift their searches toward more expensive locations on average, potentially with the aim of moving up the housing ladder. This result is consistent with collateral constraints being an important channel driving the effect of realized price growth on behavior, as we show and discuss in detail in SectionIII.
  Third, local price growth might be correlated with the supply of listings (DeFusco, Nathanson, and Zwick (2017)). If there is correlation between realized price growth in the postcodes of the buyers' residence and historical price growth in the browsed postcodes, then there could be correlation between realized price growth and the supply of listings in browsed postcodes. Such an effect might explain the results in TableII, under the alternative hypothesis that searchers are responding not to historical price growth in their postcode of residence, but rather to the increase in the supply of listings in certain postcodes they are browsing. We directly test this alternative hypothesis in TableIA.IVby expanding equation (1) to include average log growth in the supply of listings in the postcodes typically searched by local residents.23We also include as a further control growth in the number of listings in the postcode of residence for each searcher. We find that the coefficients on both controls are not statistically significant, and that their inclusion has no effect on the estimates of the coefficient capturing the effect of realized price growth on search behavior.
  Finally, some of the geographical areas identified by the year-month × area fixed effects span large territories outside the capital city of each state. To ensure that our results are driven by postcode-level variation rather than by more aggregate variation within area (for instance, variation across smaller cities and towns within a state), we restrict the sample to the three largest cities of Australia and estimate equation (1) after including year-month × city fixed effects, along with postcode or individual user fixed effects. We find that point estimates are even larger than in TableII(see TableIA.V). A one-standard-deviation increase in postcode price growth is associated with an almost 8% increase in the number of listings, postcodes, and segments visited. In spite of the much smaller sample size, the estimates remain significant.
  One of the key insights from the previous section is that realized price growth affects search behavior by increasing search breadth. What are the resulting effects on search duration and on the match rate between buyers and listings?
  On the one hand, broader searches over the same time span increase the number of explored properties and in turn the likelihood that the buyer will find a suitable match. Within traditional theoretical frameworks of housing search, this can be interpreted as akin to an increase in search intensity, which would lead to faster matches between prospective buyers and listings. On the other hand, broader searches could potentially lead to longer search spells and a lower matching frequency because by increasing the consideration set, buyers might allocate time and attention to less relevant properties, in which case they may fail to focus their search on the properties that present valuable matches.
  We first estimate equation (2) using OLS. Due to right-censoring, the OLS coefficients are likely to be biased. We therefore also use an Accelerated Failure Time (AFT) model in which we assume that the residuals follow a lognormal distribution. Our results based on the entire sample of users are reported in Panel A of TableIV. Estimates of γ are negative and highly statistically significant (standard errors are clustered by postcode), both when the specifications only include postcode fixed effects and when they include the full set of fixed effects. The OLS coefficients are larger than the coefficients from the AFT model. The estimates from the latter model and from a specification including the full set of fixed effects imply that a one-standard-deviation increase in realized local price growth leads to a 15% reduction in search duration. Given that the average search duration in the data is nine months, our estimates imply a reduction in search duration for the average user of approximately 1.3 months.
  While all of the users in our data set are actively searching for a house, we cannot be sure that they all match with a property at the end of their search. This makes interpretation of our results potentially ambiguous because some users might stop their search because they find a match while others might “drop out” of the market without a match. To overcome this limitation, we exploit a flag created by REA for a subset of users. The flag selects those users who, based on a proprietary algorithm, are considered likely to have bought a house at the end of their search. In Panel B of TableIV, we restrict our analysis to the subset of users flagged as likely to have purchased a house. We again find a drop in search duration associated with higher realized local price growth. The results are statistically significant in spite of the small sample size, and the AFT estimates of the effect are larger than those for the full sample. For this subset of users, average search duration is 10 months. Thus, for the average user, a one-standard-deviation increase in realized price growth implies a roughly 2.5 to 3 months faster match with a listing.
  In summary, higher realized price growth leads to both an increase in search breadth and faster matches for prospective buyers. These results are consistent with broader searches increasing the likelihood and potentially the quality of matches between buyers and sellers, and has important implications for the real effects of the expansion in search breadth, which we analyze in SectionIV.
  Panel A of TableVreports the results. When only including postcode fixed effects, the relationship between realized local price growth and the number of entrants is positive and statistically significant. However, the inclusion of month × area fixed effects renders the estimates insignificant. It thus appears that the effect of realized price growth on the number of entrants is driven by area-level price growth rather than postcode-specific growth. To directly test this conjecture, we estimate a specification in which we include both postcode-specific and area price growth, together with postcode fixed effects. We find that the estimate of the coefficient for postcode-specific growth is insignificant, while that for area-level growth is highly significant. Since the number of entrants is equal to zero in many postcodes and months in the data set, we also estimate equation (3) using a Poisson fixed effects model, as in Silva and Tenreyro (2006) and Cohn, Liu, and Wardlaw (2022). We obtain essentially the same results, though point estimates of the effect of realized price growth on entry are larger, as shown in TableIA.VI.
  Our results are consistent with models in which the rate at which homeowners become dissatisfied with their current house, or at which renters decide to enter the housing market, are driven by metro area-level effects, such as aggregate income shocks and population growth (see, e.g., Head, Huw, and Sun (2014)), but not by postcode-level experiences. Alternatively, our results are also consistent with a limited attention story whereby inactive homeowners and renters are not aware of price fluctuations in their postcode of residence but may still be exposed to news and sentiment about the trend of house prices in the greater metro area in which they live (Soo (2018)). For users who are currently renters, the effect can also be due to the influence of past area-level growth on beliefs about future price growth, as we discuss in detail in SectionIII.
  The effects discussed above are specific to prospectivehomebuyers$homebuyers$, that is, they are not shared by other users who are active on the website but are instead searching for a property torent$rent$. In Panel B of TableV, we reestimate equation (3) but set the dependent variable to the log number of users starting their search for a rental property. We find no relationship between realized price growth, at the level of either the postcode of residence or the entire area, and the number of users joining the sample in a specific postcode and month (the same occurs in the Poisson model; see TableIA.VI).
article.III. Economic Mechanism:
In this section, we exploit heterogeneity across prospective homebuyers to shed light on the mechanisms that drive their response to realized local price growth. We focus on two key channels: extrapolative expectations and collateral constraints.
  First, with respect to the extrapolative expectations channel, previous research shows that recent price growth affects households' belief formation about future, and even long-term, house price growth (see Case, Shiller, and Thomson (2015) and Armona, Fuster, and Zafar (2019)). When extrapolative expectations are the only channel at play in the data, predictions for the behavior of homeowners and renters are at odds. If homeowners looking for a new house interpret recent postcode growth to be a signal of higher future area-level growth (Kuchler and Zafar (2019)), they will have no reason to search either more or less intensively, since their house offers a hedge against area-wide price fluctuations (Sinai and Souleles (2005)). If instead local homeowners extrapolate futurelocalpostcode price growth, beyond that of the rest of the surrounding area, they will have an incentive to slow their search. This is because high future local growth translates into higher future capital gains compared to homeowners inother postcodeswithin the area. Since moving typically involves selling the current house, owners will delay moving and selling, to realize the high expected capital gains. Turning to local renters, if they have extrapolative beliefs, they would instead feel pressure to find a matching house quickly, before prices grow further (“fear of missing out”). Renters will be more sensitive if they interpret local price growth as a signal of area-level or aggregate price growth, since that would signal that they might be priced out of the entire market, and less sensitive if they instead interpret higher postcode-specific growth simply as a signal of higher postcode growth.
  Second, with respect to the collateral constraints channel, higher postcode-level growth is expected to be associated with a higher sales price and higher home equity for the houses of local homeowners who are planning to move. This is particularly relevant for homeowners facing collateral or liquidity constraints, since they will likely have to roll over their home equity into the down payment for a new property. Thus, constrained homeowners searching for a new property may want to take advantage of higher local prices, and of their stronger purchasing power, by searching more extensively and matching quickly with a new home.
  In the subsections below, we present results of several tests suggesting that a pure extrapolative expectations channel would be unable to fully explain heterogeneity in behavior, and that collateral constraints are an important driver of homeowners' sensitivities to local price growth. Nonetheless, in the data, beliefs and collateral constraints might jointly influence behavior. For instance, overoptimistic households might be more likely to be constrained through high-loan-to-value mortgages (Bailey et al. (2019)), and low-income and low-wealth households, who are more likely to be constrained, might have systematically different beliefs than middle class or wealthy households (Das, Kuhnen, and Nagel (2020)). Moreover, several aspects of renters' behavior are consistent with the predictions of the extrapolative expectations channel.
  Panel A of TableVIshows that postcode-level price growth has a positive and statistically significant effect on the number of listings, postcodes, and segments explored by homeowners (δown$\delta _{own}$). This is at odds with the predictions of a pure extrapolative expectations channel, which would instead predict a negative (when postcode growth is extrapolated to predict future postcode growth) or statistically insignificant (when postcode growth is extrapolated to predict future area-level growth) coefficient.25When considering renters, we find that point estimates ofδnoown$\delta _{noown}$are roughly 50% smaller than estimates ofδown$\delta _{own}$and not statistically significant.26However, the magnitudes are economically significant and the sign is consistent with the predictions of the extrapolation hypothesis: higher realized price growth leads to greater search effort in the form of larger search breadth. Moreover, when testing the difference between the estimates ofδown$\delta _{own}$andδnoown$\delta _{noown}$, we fail to reject the null that the two coefficients are the same.
  A potential concern when interpreting the estimates of the coefficients in equation (4) is that the distribution of users in the data set might be correlated with realized local price growth. For instance, users who are homeowners might be systematically more frequent in postcodes that have experienced higher growth. We show in TableIA.VIIthat this is not the case. Postcode-level price growth is not associated with local user characteristics or with the number of users active in the postcode. Since the presence of a small number of users in the postcode may make it harder to detect compositional effects, we show that this result holds both for the entire sample of postcodes included in our analysis and for a restricted sample of postcodes, where we exclude those postcodes with fewer than 10 users active throughout the year.
  To further explore the differential responses of current homeowners and renters, and the effects of the extrapolative beliefs channel on renters' behavior, we also revisit evidence on transitions into active search. As in SectionII.D, we study the likelihood of transitioning into active housing search using the number of searchers entering our sample in each month and in each postcode. We then estimate equation (3) separately for searchers who are homeowners and renters (see Panels B and C of TableVI). Higher postcode growth has no effect on entry when we control for year-month × area fixed effects or for price growth in the area. However, area-level price growth affects entry, with a positive and statistically significant sign for both groups. The effect for homeowners is again not consistent with the predictions of a pure expectations channel. In contrast, the effect for renters is consistent with predictions of the extrapolative expectation channel in the case in which renters decide to enter the housing market on the extrapolation of future area-level price growth based on historical area-level price growth (see also Agarwal, Hu, and Huang (2016)).
  The fact that homeowners increase search breadth in response to higher realized price growth is consistent with the predictions of the collateral constraints, or home equity, channel. In this section, we exploit heterogeneity across homeowners to provide further evidence on this argument.
  Estimates of the coefficients in equation (5) are reported in Panel A of TableVII. We find that in postcodes with prices below the median, a one-standard-deviation increase in realized price growth translates into more than a 14% increase in the number of browsed listings and a 10% to 11% increase in the number of postcodes and segments browsed. These results are roughly twice the magnitude of the effects for the entire sample of users, reported in TableII. The effect of realized growth in postcodes above the median is not statistically significant, and point estimates ofδphigh$\delta _{phigh}$are economically negligible. Moreover, a test for the difference between the coefficients for high and low price postcodes strongly rejects the null that the coefficients are the same. These results provide strong support for collateral constraints explaining cross-sectional differences across homeowners. However, the negligible effects for the “unconstrained” group of homeowners, living in high-price postcodes, are also potentially consistent with the predictions of extrapolative beliefs, particularly for the case in which higher local price growth is taken as a signal of higher future aggregate (area-level) price growth.
article.IV. Real Effects: Search Behavior and Sp:
In this section, we use both reduced-form analysis and a quantitative equilibrium model to investigate whether the expansion in search breadth has real effects on house sales. The unique level of detail of the data allows us to identify such spillover effects, since we observe both the postcodes from which searches originate and the postcodes to which they are directed. Moreover, these spillovers, which are determined by the behavior of local homebuyers, operate through the network of searches and thus not necessarily throughgeographicproximity$geographic\nobreakspace proximity$, which is the typical spillover channel explored by previous work in the literature.
  The expansion of search breadth can in principle lead to both positive and negative spillovers within a metropolitan area. An increase in search ranges may imply that individual listings receive attention from a broader set of buyers, which could result in faster matches, higher sales prices, and a drop in the inventory of listings for sale. Alternatively, an increase of search ranges may lead to the opposite effects. When a group of buyers focuses searches on a specific postcode or segment, they can only match with listings in that segment. If search breadth expands, the buyers will also explore listings in other parts of the metropolitan area, which could reduce match rates and prices in the original postcode or segment. These opposing mechanisms make the ultimate outcome an open question.
  We first provide reduced-form estimates showing that, by driving changes in search behavior, local price growth drives further price growth and drops in inventories in other parts of a metropolitan area, consistent with the first mechanism described above. We then use the model developed by Piazzesi, Schneider, and Stroebel (2020) to show that the magnitudes of the effects that we estimate in the data are economically sensible.
  Reduced-form tests of spillover effects face important identification challenges. Users who have observed higher price growth in their home postcode may be systematically more likely to visit higher-quality properties. These properties may therefore be selling at higher prices only due to characteristics that are not spanned by the controls available in our data set. In this case, regressing listings' sales prices on the price growth observed by the specific group of users visiting each listing could lead to biased estimates. The same concern applies to the effects on local inventories: buyers may shift their searches to higher-quality areas, which may have tighter inventories.
  Columns (3) to (8) of TableVIIIreport our estimates from the second-stage regression (equation (10)), while estimates for the first-stage regression (equation (9)) are reported in TableIA.XI. We find that estimates of γ are positive and highly significant. A 10% increase in search breadth associated with higher realized price growth in the buyers' postcodes of residence33translates into 2% to 2.5% higher sales prices. Since the average home in our sample has a sales price of approximately 750,000 Australian dollars (562,000 U.S. dollars, based on the average exchange rate over the period from January 2017 to May 2019), this effect amounts to roughly 15,000 to 18,750 Australian dollars (12,000 to 15,000 U.S. dollars).
  While the methodology used to constructΔpl$\Delta p_{l}$addresses issues related to the endogeneity of the match between listings and visitors, there may still be concerns thatΔpl$\Delta p_{l}$could be correlated with unobservable listing characteristics, and thus that our estimates in equations (8) and (10) might be biased.
  In SectionIVof theInternet Appendix, we conduct several tests showing that our results are robust to unobservables bias. In the same appendix, we also address another potential concern. If destination postcodes are relatively close to the buyers' current postcodes of residence, the relationship between experienced price growth, search breadth, and sales prices may not be driven by spillovers, but rather by common movements within a local housing market. The same issue applies to the estimates of the effects on inventories. To address this concern, we repeat our analysis after excluding from the construction ofΔpl$\Delta p_{l}$andΔpp,t−1$\Delta p_{p,t-1}$buyers who reside in postcodes that are close to the postcode in which listinglis located.34We continue to find positive and statistically significant effects of realized price growth and search breadth on sales prices and inventories.
  While the direction of the results from the reduced-form estimates is consistent with broader searches increasing match rates within a metropolitan area, we are still left with the question of whether the magnitude of our estimates is economically sensible. To address this question, we rely on a quantitative exercise based on the structural equilibrium framework developed by Piazzesi, Schneider, and Stroebel (2020), calibrated to data on search behavior, listings, and house prices for the metropolitan areas of Melbourne and Sydney.
  In the model, the housing market of a metropolitan area is divided into “segments,” and buyers differ by search breadth, with buyers of different “types” exploring different subsets of segments. For simplicity, in the model we set segments equal to postcodes. We then use the data on search behavior to construct the distribution of buyers across types for each of the two metropolitan areas. Finally, we use information on postcode-level listings' turnover, inventories, and sales prices to fully calibrate the model.35A detailed description of the model is in SectionV.Aof theInternet Appendix, while information on the calibration is in SectionV.Bof theInternet Appendix.
  After having calibrated the model separately to the data for Melbourne and Sydney, we conduct our quantitative exercise. In the exercise, we change buyers' search breadth and then study changes in listings' inventories and prices, both at the metropolitan area level and across postcodes within each metropolitan area. In particular, we modify the distribution of buyer types so that the average search breadth, measured as the average number of postcodes explored by buyers, increases by 5%, consistent with the increase generated by a one-standard-deviation increase in local realized price growth in our empirical estimates (see SectionV.Cof theInternet Appendix).
  The results in SectionII.Cindicate that broader searches induced by higher experienced price growth also lead to shorter search duration. In particular, a one-standard-deviation increase in realized price growth in the postcode of residence coincides with a 15% reduction in search duration. Note that this effect cannot be replicated simply by increasing search breadth in the baseline model, due to the assumption of random matching between buyers and sellers. Thus, to replicate the drop in search duration that we observe in the data, we also change buyers' matching rates, by an amount proportional to the change in the fraction of broad searchers present in each postcode. This change is calibrated so that on average the matching rate for buyers across postcodes increases by approximately 15% to 16% in Melbourne and Sydney, in line with the estimates from the data (see SectionV.Cof theInternet Appendixfor more details).
  TableXshows the change in market outcomes induced by the change in search breadth and duration. We report the mean, median, and 25thand 75thpercentiles of the change in the distribution of prices, inventories, and buyers' matching rates across postcodes within Melbourne and Sydney. Postcode prices increase on average by roughly 1.1% in Melbourne and 1.9% in Sydney, and inventories decrease by 14.4% and 15.6%, respectively. Note that there is substantial cross-sectional variation across postcodes. The 25thpercentiles for the change in prices and inventory across Sydney's postcodes are 1.45% and 14.55%, while the 75thpercentiles are 2.2% and 17.8%. The average increase in buyers' match rates is roughly 15% in Melbourne and 16.3% in Sydney.
  Even though search breadth increases by 5% on average in each metropolitan area, due to the uneven structure of the network of searchers, there is cross-sectional variation in the postcode-level change in search breadth. Figure4shows that there is a clear positive relation between the postcode-level increase in search breadth and the change in prices, and a negative relation between the increase in search breadth and the change in inventory, consistent with the reduced-form estimates in the previous section. For example, within Sydney, a postcode that experiences a 10% higher increase in the search breadth of its visitors has a 1.5% larger increase in prices and a 6% larger drop in inventory. Overall, the magnitudes generated by the model line up with the reduced-form estimates from the data presented in the previous section.
  We should highlight, that, while strong, the positive relationship between search breadth and prices, and the negative relationship between search breadth and inventories, is not mechanical. This is because there are two forces at work in the model, which mimic the channels at play in the data. On the one hand, the increase in search breadth leads to higher match rates for buyers. On the other hand, it makes buyers' searches more widespread, which, given the random matching nature of the model, has ambiguous effects. Consider, for instance, the extreme case of a metropolitan area in which all buyers are narrow types, searching only in one postcode. Then all of these buyers will match with probability one with a listing in their chosen postcode (their “narrow market”). An increase in search breadth implies that buyers may now also purchase in other postcodes, increasing “demand” in these postcodes and leading in turn to higher sales volume, higher prices, and lower inventory. The increase in search breadth also has the opposite effect: local buyers now have search ranges that span multiple postcodes, and the likelihood of them matching with a listing in their original narrow market is now smaller than one.
  It is intuitive to expect that the increase in search breadth will also lead to a decrease in the dispersion of prices across postcodes. Indeed, perfect market integration would lead to uniform search ranges, covering the entire metropolitan area, and would eliminate any heterogeneity driven by differences in buyer types across postcodes. However, these second-moment effects are small compared to the effects on price levels and inventories.36
article.V. Concluding Remarks:
We show that differences in local realized price growth explain differences in search behavior of local households and lead to spillovers on house sales prices and listing inventories within a metropolitan area. Since the housing market is highly segmented and illiquid, search behavior is a key determinant of the likelihood of matches between buyers and sellers and thus of house sales. While previous work documents how local realized price growth affects house price expectations and local homeowners' collateral constraints, there is virtually no empirical evidence on the effects on search behavior of prospective homebuyers. We provide a novel contribution by taking advantage of a unique data set that tracks prospective homebuyers' activity on a major property website.
  Higher recent postcode-level price growth increases the set of properties included in house searches, and the number of locations and segments (combinations of locations and property characteristics) browsed, but has no effect on the attention allocated to exploring individual listings. The expansion of the breadth of searches also coincides with a decrease in search duration, consistent with broader consideration sets leading to a higher likelihood of buyers matching with listings. Exploiting heterogeneity across users, we test the competing implications of two mechanisms that may explain the link between realized price growth and behavior: extrapolative expectations and collateral constraints. We find strong evidence in support of the collateral constraints channel for homeowners, and evidence consistent with a role for the extrapolative expectations channel for renters.
  In the last part of the paper, we focus on the real effects of changes in search behavior. We first construct reduced-form tests and find that the expansion of buyers' search breadth leads to an increase in prices and a drop in listing inventories in destination postcodes. We then show that broader search breadth, when paired with an increase in buyers' matching rates with listings, has effects of the same sign and magnitude in a quantitative equilibrium model with segmented search that is calibrated to the data. Since the response of searchers depends on their characteristics, and the propagation of spillovers operates through the network of searches, these findings shed light on how search data can be used to explain heterogeneous price patterns within a metropolitan area and on potential feedback effects across locations, independent of proximity.
  Taken together, our results show how differences in local price experiences affect differences in behavior across households searching for a house, and in turn prices and inventories. Our findings may also apply to other search markets, potentially explaining differences in search behavior, and related price effects, more broadly in the economy.
  Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
